topic: Code purpose and scope
content: Part A of the Red Ensign Group Yacht Code provides design criteria, construction standards and other safety measures for yachts carrying no more than 12 passengers so as to minimise risk to the yacht, persons onboard and the environment. The Code applies to motor or sailing vessels of 24 metres load line length and over, or if built before 21 July 1968, 150GT or over, in commercial use for sport or pleasure, carrying no cargo and no more than 12 passengers.

topic: Machinery modifications must meet MARPOL Annex VI
content: Where repair, alteration or modification substantially alters yacht dimensions or service life, the affected areas are treated as for a new vessel. Replacement, addition or modification of propulsion and auxiliary machinery is to comply with the applicable requirements of MARPOL Annex VI. Where a change in vessel particulars reaches a new regulatory threshold, such as 500GT or over 85 metres load line length, compliance with the relevant provisions applicable to a new vessel of that size is required.

topic: Servicing intervals for life-saving appliances
content: Every inflatable lifejacket, inflatable liferaft, marine evacuation system and hydrostatic release unit, other than a disposable hydrostatic release unit, shall be serviced at intervals not exceeding 12 months unless extended service intervals have been approved by the Administration, and servicing shall be at a manufacturer-approved service station. In addition to servicing intervals, each marine evacuation system shall be deployed from the ship on a rotational basis at intervals agreed by the Administration, and each system shall be deployed at least once every six years.

topic: Safety management for vessels under 500GT
content: All vessels under 500GT shall employ a safety management system as described in Chapter 23A. All vessels shall include emergency towing procedures, as required by SOLAS II-1/3-4, in their safety management system or in a separate plan. The purpose of Chapter 23A is to provide guidance on how to develop and implement an effective safety management system for vessels under 500GT where full certification to the ISM Code is not required. The company or owner should create a safe working environment and have a health and safety protection policy covering health, safety and the environment for staff ashore and afloat.

topic: Emergencies, accident reporting, maintenance and review under 500GT SMS
content: Emergency exercises should involve personnel ashore and afloat where possible, with roles and responsibilities defined and exercises recorded. Vessels operating under the Code shall report any accidents to the Administration and the company shall therefore have a procedure in place. All accidents and near accidents shall be recorded and reported to the company or owner, who shall implement corrective action aimed at improving safety. Maintenance of the vessel and equipment is an essential ingredient of safety management. Equipment shall be checked and tested daily when in use, there shall be procedures for more detailed inspection and maintenance, and every inspection event shall be recorded. Every company or owner shall undertake a review of the safety management system of all vessels at least once every three years.

topic: ISM requirement for vessels of 500GT and over
content: Every ship to which Chapter 23B applies, meaning vessels of 500GT and over, shall comply with the applicable requirements of SOLAS chapter IX, the International Safety Management Code. All vessels shall include emergency towing procedures, as required by SOLAS II-1/3-4, in their safety management system or in a separate plan.

topic: Operational readiness, maintenance and inspections
content: Chapter 26 covers operational readiness, maintenance and inspections. Vessels shall comply with the requirements of Annex I and those of the Administration. For AskHelm routing, questions about readiness, inspection routines and maintenance status should be treated as operational compliance questions under the Yacht Code.

topic: Observation, non-conformity and major non-conformity
content: Observation means a statement of fact made during a safety management audit and substantiated by objective evidence. Non-conformity means an observed situation where objective evidence indicates the non-fulfilment of a specified requirement. Major non-conformity means an identifiable deviation that poses a serious threat to the safety of personnel or the ship, or a serious risk to the environment, that requires immediate corrective action, or the lack of effective and systematic implementation of a requirement of the Code.

topic: Objectives of the ISM Code
content: The objectives of the ISM Code are to ensure safety at sea, prevention of human injury or loss of life, and avoidance of damage to the environment, in particular the marine environment, and to property. Safety management objectives should provide safe practices in ship operation and a safe working environment, assess all identified risks to ships, personnel and the environment and establish appropriate safeguards, and continuously improve safety management skills of personnel ashore and aboard ships, including preparing for emergencies related both to safety and environmental protection. The safety management system should ensure compliance with mandatory rules and regulations and that applicable codes, guidelines and standards recommended by IMO, Administrations, classification societies and maritime industry organizations are taken into account.

topic: What an SMS must contain
content: Every company should develop, implement and maintain a safety management system that includes a safety and environmental-protection policy; instructions and procedures to ensure safe operation of ships and protection of the environment in compliance with relevant international and flag State legislation; defined levels of authority and lines of communication between shore and shipboard personnel; procedures for reporting accidents and non-conformities; procedures to prepare for and respond to emergency situations; and procedures for internal audits and management reviews. The company should establish a safety and environmental-protection policy describing how the Code objectives will be achieved and ensure it is implemented and maintained at all levels of the organization, both ship-based and shore-based.

topic: Company responsibilities, DPA and master authority
content: The company should define and document the responsibility, authority and interrelation of all personnel who manage, perform and verify work relating to safety and pollution prevention. The company is responsible for ensuring adequate resources and shore-based support are provided to enable the designated person to carry out their functions. Every company should designate person or persons ashore with direct access to the highest level of management to monitor the safety and pollution-prevention aspects of each ship and ensure adequate resources and shore-based support. The company should clearly define and document the master’s responsibility, including implementing the company policy, motivating the crew, issuing clear orders, verifying requirements are observed, periodically reviewing the SMS and reporting deficiencies. The SMS must emphasise the master’s overriding authority and responsibility to make decisions with respect to safety and pollution prevention and to request the company’s assistance as necessary.

topic: Resources, qualifications, familiarization and training
content: The company should ensure the master is properly qualified for command, fully conversant with the SMS and given the necessary support to perform duties safely. Each ship should be manned with qualified, certificated and medically fit seafarers in accordance with national and international requirements and appropriately manned to maintain safe operations. The company should establish procedures to ensure new personnel and personnel transferred to new assignments related to safety and environmental protection are properly familiarized with their duties before sailing where essential. Personnel involved in the SMS should understand relevant rules, regulations, codes and guidelines, receive required training, receive relevant information in working languages understood by them, and be able to communicate effectively in execution of their duties.

topic: Shipboard operations, emergencies, reporting and maintenance
content: The company should establish procedures, plans and instructions, including checklists as appropriate, for key shipboard operations concerning the safety of personnel, the ship and protection of the environment, with tasks defined and assigned to qualified personnel. The company should identify potential emergency shipboard situations, establish procedures to respond to them, establish programmes for drills and exercises and ensure the company organization can respond at any time to hazards, accidents and emergency situations involving its ships. The SMS should include procedures ensuring that non-conformities, accidents and hazardous situations are reported to the company, investigated and analysed with the objective of improving safety and pollution prevention, and should include procedures for corrective action including measures to prevent recurrence. The company should establish procedures to ensure the ship is maintained in conformity with relevant rules and regulations and any additional company requirements.

document: ISM Code / SOLAS IX extract (2018 Edition)
topic: DOC, SMC, verification and maintenance of conditions
content: The company and the ship shall comply with the requirements of the ISM Code, and the ship shall be operated by a company holding a Document of Compliance. A Document of Compliance shall be issued to every company complying with the Code, and a Safety Management Certificate shall be issued to every ship after the Administration or recognized organization verifies that the company and its shipboard management operate in accordance with the approved safety management system. The safety management system shall be maintained in accordance with the provisions of the Code. The Administration or a recognized organization shall periodically verify the proper functioning of the ship’s safety management system, and ships required to hold a certificate are subject to control under the relevant SOLAS verification provisions.
source: ISM Code / SOLAS IX extract (2018 Edition)

topic: Acceptance and entry into force dates
content: The MEPC.392(82) amendments to MARPOL Annex VI concerning the Canadian Arctic and Norwegian Sea emission control areas were deemed accepted on 1 September 2025 unless sufficient objections were received, and they entered into force on 1 March 2026.

topic: NOx Tier III in Norwegian Sea ECA
content: For the Norwegian Sea Emission Control Area, MARPOL Annex VI regulation 13 adds NOx Tier III applicability when a ship is constructed on or after 1 March 2026 and is operating in the Norwegian Sea ECA. For this area, “ship constructed on or after 1 March 2026” means a ship for which the building contract is placed on or after 1 March 2026, or in the absence of a building contract the keel is laid or the ship is at a similar stage of construction on or after 1 September 2026, or the delivery is on or after 1 March 2030.

topic: NOx Tier III in Canadian Arctic ECA
content: For the Canadian Arctic Emission Control Area, MARPOL Annex VI regulation 13 adds NOx Tier III applicability where a ship is constructed on or after 1 January 2025 and is operating in the Canadian Arctic ECA.

topic: New ECA designations under Annex VI
content: MARPOL Annex VI regulation 13.6 adds the Canadian Arctic Emission Control Area and the Norwegian Sea as emission control areas for nitrogen oxides. Regulation 14.3 also adds the Canadian Arctic Emission Control Area and the Norwegian Sea to the list of areas where sulphur oxides and particulate matter emission control area requirements apply.

topic: IAPP certificate build-date fields
content: The Supplement to the International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate was amended so that section 1.3 records the date of build using three specific dates: the date of building contract, the date on which the keel was laid or ship was at a similar stage of construction, and the date of delivery.

topic: Canadian Arctic ECA boundary summary
content: Appendix VII states that the Canadian Arctic emission control area comprises two coordinate-defined segments. The first segment starts at the Yukon mainland at 68°54.00 N, 137°0.00 W and ends at the north coast of Hans Island at 80°49.91 N, 66°27.40 W. The second segment continues from the south coast of Hans Island at 80°49.29 N, 66°27.04 W and ends at the coast of Newfoundland and Labrador at 60°0.00 N, 64°9.60 W. For AskHelm, the detailed coordinates should be treated as a boundary reference source rather than quoted in full in every answer.

topic: ISM Code Chapter 1 — definitions company SMS DOC SMC non-conformity major non-conformity
content:
ISM Code Chapter 1 — Definitions.

Company: The owner of the ship or any other organisation or person such as the manager or bareboat charterer who has assumed responsibility for operation of the ship from the shipowner.

Safety management system (SMS): A structured and documented system enabling company personnel to implement effectively the company safety and environmental protection policy.

Document of Compliance (DOC): A document issued to a company complying with the requirements of the ISM Code.

Safety Management Certificate (SMC): A document issued to a ship certifying that the company and its shipboard management operate in accordance with an approved SMS.

Non-conformity: An observed situation where objective evidence indicates the non-fulfilment of a specified requirement.

Major non-conformity: An identifiable deviation that poses a serious threat to the safety of personnel or the ship or a serious risk to the environment that requires immediate corrective action.

Observation: A statement of fact made during a safety management audit and substantiated by objective evidence.

topic: ISM Code Chapter 1.2 — objectives safety at sea pollution prevention human injury
content:
ISM Code Chapter 1.2 — Objectives.

The objectives of the ISM Code are to ensure safety at sea, prevention of human injury or loss of life, and avoidance of damage to the environment, in particular the marine environment, and to property.

Safety management objectives of the company should:
1. provide for safe practices in ship operation and a safe working environment;
2. assess all identified risks to its ships, personnel and the environment and establish appropriate safeguards; and
3. continuously improve safety management skills of personnel ashore and aboard ships, including preparing for emergencies related both to safety and environmental protection.

The ISM Code requires the SMS to ensure:
.1 compliance with mandatory rules and regulations; and
.2 that applicable codes, guidelines and standards recommended by the Organization, Administrations, classification societies and maritime industry organisations are taken into account.

section: Chapter 2 — Safety and Environmental Protection Policy
topic: ISM Code Chapter 2 — safety policy environmental protection policy implementation
content:
ISM Code Chapter 2 — Safety and Environmental Protection Policy.

2.1 The Company shall establish a safety and environmental protection policy which describes how the objectives given in paragraph 1.2 will be achieved.

2.2 The company shall ensure that the policy is implemented and maintained at all levels of the organisation, both ship-based and shore-based.
source_reference: ISM Code 2018 — Chapter 2, Safety and Environmental Protection Policy

topic: ISM Code Chapter 3 — company responsibilities authority SMS resources shore support
content:
ISM Code Chapter 3 — Company Responsibilities and Authority.

3.1 If the entity who is responsible for the operation of the ship is other than the owner, the owner must report the full name and details of such entity to the Administration.

3.2 The company shall define and document the responsibility, authority and interrelation of all personnel who manage, perform and verify work relating to and affecting safety and pollution prevention.

3.3 The company is responsible for ensuring that adequate resources and shore-based support are provided to enable the Designated Person or Persons to carry out their functions.
source_reference: ISM Code 2018 — Chapter 3, Company Responsibilities and Authority

topic: ISM Code Chapter 4 — designated person ashore DPA safety link management access
content:
ISM Code Chapter 4 — Designated Person(s).

To ensure the safe operation of each ship and to provide a link between the company and those on board, every company, as appropriate, should designate a person or persons ashore having direct access to the highest level of management.

The responsibility and authority of the Designated Person or Persons shall include monitoring the safety and pollution prevention aspects of the operation of each ship and ensuring that adequate resources and shore-based support are applied, as required.

topic: ISM Code Chapter 5 — master responsibility authority overriding authority safety decisions
content:
ISM Code Chapter 5 — Master's Responsibility and Authority.

5.1 The company shall clearly define and document the master's responsibility with regard to implementing the safety and environmental protection policy of the company.

5.2 The company shall ensure that the SMS operated on board the ship contains a clear statement emphasising the master's authority. The company shall establish in the SMS that the master has the overriding authority and the responsibility to make decisions with respect to safety and pollution prevention and to request the company's assistance as may be necessary.
source_reference: ISM Code 2018 — Chapter 5, Master's Responsibility and Authority

topic: ISM Code Chapter 6 — resources personnel qualifications training familiarisation crew
content:
ISM Code Chapter 6 — Resources and Personnel.

6.1 The company shall ensure that the master is: properly qualified for command; fully conversant with the company's SMS; and given the necessary support so that the master's duties can be safely performed.

6.2 The company shall ensure that each ship is manned with qualified, certificated and medically fit seafarers in accordance with national and international requirements.

6.3 The company shall establish procedures to ensure that new personnel and personnel transferred to new assignments related to safety and protection of the environment are given proper familiarisation with their duties.

6.4 The company shall ensure that all personnel involved in the company's SMS have an adequate understanding of relevant rules, regulations, codes and guidelines.

6.5 The company shall establish and maintain procedures for identifying any training which may be required in support of the SMS and ensure that such training is provided for all personnel concerned.

6.6 The company shall establish procedures by which the ship's personnel receive relevant information on the SMS in a working language or languages understood by them.

6.7 The company shall ensure that the ship's personnel are able to communicate effectively in the execution of their duties related to the SMS.
source_reference: ISM Code 2018 — Chapter 6, Resources and Personnel

topic: ISM Code Chapter 7 — shipboard operations procedures plans instructions checklists key operations
content:
ISM Code Chapter 7 — Shipboard Operations.

The company shall establish procedures, plans and instructions, including checklists as appropriate, for key shipboard operations concerning the safety of personnel, the ship and protection of the environment. The various tasks involved shall be defined and assigned to qualified personnel.

topic: ISM Code Chapter 8 — emergency preparedness drills exercises muster emergency response
content:
ISM Code Chapter 8 — Emergency Preparedness.

8.1 The company shall establish procedures to identify, describe and respond to potential emergency shipboard situations.

8.2 The company shall establish programmes for drills and exercises to prepare for emergency actions.

8.3 The SMS shall provide for measures ensuring that the company's organisation can respond at any time to hazards, accidents and emergency situations involving its ships.

section: Chapter 9 — Reports and Analysis of Non-conformities
topic: ISM Code Chapter 9 — non-conformities accidents hazardous occurrences reporting investigation corrective action
content:
ISM Code Chapter 9 — Reports and Analysis of Non-conformities, Accidents and Hazardous Occurrences.

9.1 The SMS shall include procedures ensuring that non-conformities, accidents and hazardous situations are reported to the company, investigated and analysed with the objective of improving safety and pollution prevention.

9.2 The company shall establish procedures for the implementation of corrective action, including measures intended to prevent recurrence.
source_reference: ISM Code 2018 — Chapter 9, Reports and Analysis

section: Chapter 10 — Maintenance of the Ship and Equipment
topic: ISM Code Chapter 10 — maintenance ship equipment inspections non-conformity corrective action records critical equipment standby testing reliability
content:
ISM Code Chapter 10 — Maintenance of the Ship and Equipment.

10.1 The company shall establish procedures to ensure that the ship is maintained in conformity with the provisions of the relevant rules and regulations and with any additional requirements which may be established by the Company.

10.2 In meeting these requirements the Company shall ensure that:
.1 inspections are held at appropriate intervals;
.2 any non-conformity is reported, with its possible cause, if known;
.3 corrective action is taken; and
.4 records of these activities are maintained.

10.3 The company shall establish procedures in its SMS to identify equipment and technical systems the sudden operational failure of which may result in hazardous situations. The SMS shall provide for specific measures aimed at promoting the reliability of such equipment or systems. These measures shall include the regular testing of stand-by arrangements and equipment or technical systems that are not in continuous use.
source_reference: ISM Code 2018 — Chapter 10, Maintenance of the Ship and Equipment

topic: ISM Code Chapter 11 — documentation records SMS documents controlled safety management manual
content:
ISM Code Chapter 11 — Documentation.

11.1 The company shall establish and maintain procedures to control all documents and data which are relevant to the SMS.

11.2 The company shall ensure that:
.1 valid documents are available at all relevant locations;
.2 changes to documents are reviewed and approved by authorised personnel; and
.3 obsolete documents are promptly removed.

11.3 The documents used to describe and implement the SMS may be referred to as the Safety Management Manual. Documentation should be kept in a form that the company considers most effective. Each ship should carry on board all documentation relevant to that ship.

section: Chapter 12 — Company Verification, Review and Evaluation
topic: ISM Code Chapter 12 — internal audit company verification review evaluation SMS effectiveness
content:
ISM Code Chapter 12 — Company Verification, Review and Evaluation.

12.1 The company shall carry out internal safety audits to verify whether safety and pollution prevention activities comply with the SMS.

12.2 The company shall periodically evaluate the efficiency of and, when needed, review the SMS in accordance with procedures established by the company.

12.3 The audits and possible corrective actions shall be carried out in accordance with documented procedures.

12.4 Personnel carrying out audits shall be independent of the areas being audited, unless this is impracticable due to the size and the nature of the company.

12.5 The results of the audits and reviews shall be brought to the attention of all personnel having responsibility in the area involved.

12.6 The management personnel responsible for the area involved shall take timely corrective action on deficiencies found.
source_reference: ISM Code 2018 — Chapter 12, Company Verification, Review and Evaluation

section: Annex I — OWS, OCM and bilge water discharge
topic: MARPOL Annex I OWS oily water separator oil content monitor OCM 15ppm bilge water discharge machinery space
source_reference: MARPOL Annex I — Regulations 14–15, OWS and Bilge Water

topic: MARPOL Annex I Oil Record Book ORB entries records machinery space 3 years retention
source_reference: MARPOL Annex I — Regulation 17, Oil Record Book

topic: MARPOL Annex IV sewage discharge holding tank treatment plant 3 nm 12 nm Baltic special area
content:
MARPOL Annex IV — Prevention of Pollution by Sewage from Ships.

Applies to ships of 400 GT and above, and ships certified to carry more than 15 persons.

Regulation 11 — Discharge of sewage:
Discharge of sewage into the sea is prohibited unless:
• Ship has in operation an approved sewage treatment plant AND is more than 3 nm from nearest land (treated sewage); OR
• Sewage is comminuted and disinfected AND the ship is more than 12 nm from nearest land; OR
• Sewage comes from a holding tank and is discharged at moderate rate while underway AND the ship is more than 12 nm from nearest land.

Special area — Baltic Sea: Passenger ships must not discharge sewage into the Baltic regardless of distance from land. They must use port reception facilities or an approved sewage treatment plant meeting the stricter Baltic standards.

Required equipment: Sewage treatment plant, or comminuting and disinfecting system, or sewage holding tank with adequate capacity for all sewage while in port, at anchor, or within restricted areas.

topic: MARPOL Annex V garbage plastics food waste garbage management plan garbage record book discharge prohibited
content:
MARPOL Annex V — Prevention of Pollution by Garbage from Ships.

Regulation 3 — Discharge of garbage outside special areas:
ALL plastics — including synthetic ropes, fishing gear, plastic bags and incinerator ashes from plastic products — may NEVER be discharged into the sea.

Food waste may be discharged when:
• More than 3 nm from nearest land if passed through a comminuter/grinder (< 25 mm); OR
• More than 12 nm from nearest land if not comminuted or ground.

Cargo residues, cleaning agents and additives, animal carcasses: subject to specific rules.

Regulation 10 — Garbage management plan and record:
Every ship of 100 GT and above, and every ship certified to carry 15 or more persons, shall carry:
• A Garbage Management Plan (specifying procedures for collection, storage, processing and disposal, and designating a person in charge); and
• A Garbage Record Book (recording all discharges to sea or reception facility, and all completed incinerations).

Special areas (Baltic, Black Sea, Mediterranean, Red Sea, Gulfs, North Sea, Antarctic, Wider Caribbean): In special areas only food waste may be discharged, and only when more than 12 nm from nearest land.

topic: MARPOL Annex VI sulphur SOx limit 0.5% 0.10% ECA emission control area SECA scrubber low sulphur fuel
content:
MARPOL Annex VI — Sulphur Oxide Emissions (Regulation 14).

Global sulphur cap: Since 1 January 2020, the sulphur content of fuel oil used on board any ship must not exceed 0.50% m/m.

Emission Control Areas (ECAs) — sulphur limit: The sulphur content of fuel oil used on board ships in an ECA must not exceed 0.10% m/m.

Established SOx ECAs: Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American ECA (including US coastal waters), US Caribbean Sea ECA.

Compliance options:
• Use compliant low-sulphur fuel oil (LSFO/MGO) meeting the applicable limit;
• Fit an approved exhaust gas cleaning system (scrubber) that achieves equivalent emission reductions — evidence of compliance must be maintained on board.

Fuel changeover: When entering or leaving an ECA, a written fuel changeover procedure must be followed and the changeover (date, time, position, fuel grade, tank volumes) recorded in the logbook.
source_reference: MARPOL Annex VI — Regulation 14, Sulphur Emissions

topic: MARPOL Annex VI NOx Tier I Tier II Tier III ECA North American Baltic Norwegian
content:
MARPOL Annex VI — NOx Emissions from Diesel Engines (Regulation 13).

Tier I: Applies to marine diesel engines installed on ships constructed on or after 1 January 2000.

Tier II: Applies to marine diesel engines installed on ships constructed on or after 1 January 2011. Approximately 15–20% lower NOx limit than Tier I.

Tier III: Applies in NOx Tier III Emission Control Areas (NECAs) for marine diesel engines installed on ships constructed on or after 1 January 2016. Approximately 80% lower NOx limit than Tier I.

Established NOx ECAs (NECAs): North American ECA (including US coastal waters), US Caribbean Sea ECA, Baltic Sea ECA (from 1 January 2021), North Sea ECA (from 1 January 2021).

Norwegian Sea ECA: Under MEPC.392(82), adopted 2024, the Norwegian Sea area around Svalbard is designated as a NOx Tier III ECA.
source_reference: MARPOL Annex VI — Regulation 13, NOx Emissions

section: Annex VI — IAPP Certificate and fuel record keeping
topic: MARPOL Annex VI IAPP certificate fuel oil record keeping BDN bunker delivery note sampling 3 years
content:
MARPOL Annex VI — IAPP Certificate and Fuel Oil Record Keeping.

IAPP Certificate (Regulation 6): Ships of 400 GT and above must hold a valid International Air Pollution Prevention (IAPP) Certificate.

Bunker Delivery Note (Regulation 18): A BDN must be provided for every fuel oil delivery. The BDN must be retained on board for at least 3 years after delivery.

MARPOL sample: A representative sample of fuel oil delivered must be retained on board for at least 12 months from the date of delivery.

Log records: Date, time, position, and fuel grade changeover details must be recorded in the official log book on ECA entry and exit.

EEDI / SEEMP (Regulations 20–22): Ships of 400 GT and above must have a Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP) on board. New ships are subject to the Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI).
source_reference: MARPOL Annex VI — IAPP Certificate and Fuel Records
